name: PLC Firmware Implant (Stuxnet-Style)
description: Implant malicious firmware into Programmable Logic Controllers to covertly manipulate industrial processes while reporting normal telemetry — inspired by Stuxnet, TRITON, and Industroyer methodologies.
root_objective: Implant malicious PLC firmware to covertly manipulate industrial processes

nodes.title: Implant Persistent Malicious PLC Logic
nodes.description: Upload modified PLC firmware or logic that subtly alters process parameters while replaying expected telemetry values to the SCADA/DCS, making the manipulation invisible to operators.

nodes.title: Gain Access to Engineering Workstation
nodes.description: Compromise the engineering workstation (EWS) used to programme PLCs — the primary tool for uploading logic and firmware updates.

nodes.title: Develop and Deploy Weaponised PLC Payload
nodes.description: Create a modified PLC programme that both manipulates the target process and conceals the changes from operator view.

nodes.title: Supply Chain Compromise of EWS Software
nodes.description: Trojanise the PLC programming software installer (e.g., Siemens TIA Portal, Rockwell Studio 5000) or its update mechanism to deploy malware onto engineering workstations.

nodes.title: USB Infection via Removable Media
nodes.description: Plant a weaponised USB drive containing the PLC rootkit installer, targeting air-gapped OT networks (Stuxnet propagation method).

nodes.title: Compromise EWS via IT/OT Network Pivot
nodes.description: Traverse from the corporate IT network through the DMZ to the OT engineering VLAN where engineering workstations reside.

nodes.title: Reverse-Engineer Target PLC Firmware
nodes.description: Extract and reverse-engineer the PLC firmware to understand the proprietary instruction set, memory layout, and process-specific logic blocks.

nodes.title: Develop Man-in-the-Middle PLC Rootkit
nodes.description: Create a rootkit that intercepts read requests from the SCADA/HMI and returns pre-recorded 'normal' process values while the actual process parameters are being manipulated.

nodes.title: Upload Weaponised Logic to Target PLCs
nodes.description: Use the compromised engineering workstation to download the malicious PLC programme to the target controllers during a maintenance window to avoid detection.

nodes.title: Access to Identical PLC Hardware for Testing
nodes.description: Attacker must obtain identical PLC hardware (same model, firmware version) to build and test the weaponised payload in a lab environment before deployment.

nodes.title: Detailed Knowledge of Target Process
nodes.description: Attacker must understand the specific industrial process (setpoints, operating ranges, safety thresholds) to craft a payload that causes damage within believable parameters.

nodes.title: PLC Firmware Lacks Code Signing Verification
nodes.description: Many legacy PLCs accept firmware uploads without cryptographic verification, allowing arbitrary code execution on the controller.
